summary: Gruber covers Apple's simultaneous launch of the M5 chip across three products: the 14-inch MacBook Pro, iPad Pro, and Vision Pro. He characterizes all three as speed bump upgrades, noting the base 14-inch MacBook Pro's awkward positioning as a 'Pro' machine that most buyers would be better served getting as an Air. The iPad Pro upgrade is notable more for its N1 wireless chip (Wi-Fi 7) and C1X modem than the M5 itself. The Vision Pro gets the most dramatic jump (M2 to M5) plus a new Dual Knit Band that tacitly acknowledges comfort problems, but sees no price drop or form factor change. Gruber observes that the M5 Pro and Max models are conspicuously absent, likely coming in early 2026.
key_points: The base 14-inch MacBook Pro with the plain M-series chip occupies an awkward 'Pro but not really pro' position that dates back to the Intel era, serving buyers who would likely be better off with a MacBook Air.
Unlike previous M-series generations, the plain M5 launches without the Pro and Max variants, which are expected in early 2026.
The iPad Pro's N1 wireless chip (Wi-Fi 7) and C1X modem are more interesting upgrades than the M5 chip itself — and notably the new MacBook Pro doesn't get Wi-Fi 7.
Vision Pro's jump from M2 to M5 is the most dramatic improvement of the three, with 10% more rendered pixels and 120Hz refresh for passthrough, plus a new comfort-focused Dual Knit Band.
Regular speed bumps, even if unglamorous, are preferable to years-long gaps between hardware refreshes.
money_quotes: The base 14-inch model, with the no-adjective M-series chip, is for people who probably would be better served with a MacBook Air but who wrongly believe they 'need' a laptop with 'Pro' in its name.
It's more like a MacBook 'Pro' than a MacBook Pro.
Annual — or at least regular — speed bumps are a good thing. The alternative is years-long gaps between hardware refreshes.
I think the N1 wireless chip and C1X modem are more interesting generation-over-generation improvements than the M5 chip.
It's a tacit acknowledgement that physical comfort has been a real problem for many people who've tried Vision Pro.
Translation: Hey, there's actually a growing library of immersive content to watch, software to use, and games to play for this thing now.
It's merely another speed bump upgrade alongside the other two speed bump upgrades today, but a bit more dramatic given that the Vision Pro is jumping from the M2 to M5.
No price drop, no change to the form factor. But Apple's interest in the platform is very much alive.
key_insight: Apple's triple M5 speed bump across MacBook Pro, iPad Pro, and Vision Pro is unglamorous but strategically sound, with the most interesting details in the peripherals — Wi-Fi 7's selective appearance and Vision Pro's comfort-acknowledging Dual Knit Band — rather than the chip itself.
